title: The tailor who measured more than your shoulders
summary: Reflect on a fading craft — a tailor, cobbler, or seamstress — whose work depended on a kind of attention that machines and chain stores cannot replace.
topic: A craftsperson whose attention to the customer cannot be reproduced by a chain store
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a tailor, cobbler, watch repairer, or similar craftsperson you have known, and explain why the attention they paid to a customer is hard to reproduce on a larger scale. Use the past perfect, present perfect for reflection, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: The first time I went to Bác Tâm's tailor shop in the old quarter of Hanoi, I had expected to be measured the way I had been measured in every chain store I had been to before, but…
If you asked me what made the difference between his work and the shirts I had been buying for ten years, I would say it was not the cloth or the cut but…
Although it is easy to dismiss fading crafts as nostalgia, what I have come to think they preserve is something quietly practical that the larger system has forgotten…

hintVocab.meaningVi: kiểu cắt của áo sơ mi
một nghề thủ công đang mai một

sampleText:
The first time I went to Bác Tâm's tailor shop in the old quarter of Hanoi, I had expected to be measured the way I had been measured in every chain store I had visited before — quickly, in three places, with my arms held out and a polite, slightly bored shop assistant making a few small marks on a card. Bác Tâm took almost forty minutes. He measured my shoulders three times, in three slightly different positions, asked me to walk across the room twice, and then asked, in slow English, what kind of work I usually did with my arms during the day. When I said I taught and wrote, he wrote down something on a small piece of paper that did not look like a measurement at all. Two weeks later, when I picked up the shirt, it fitted in a way I had never experienced before. The right shoulder was very slightly higher than the left, exactly the way my own shoulder is. He had compensated for a difference I had not even known about myself.

What I have come to understand is that Bác Tâm's measurements were not really measurements in the chain-store sense; they were a slow form of observation of who I actually was. He had measured my shoulders, but he had also measured how I stood, how I moved my arms when I spoke, and the way my collar would sit if I tilted my head down to write. None of this could have been captured by a quick scan at a kiosk. Although a modern body-scanner can record dimensions far more precisely than a measuring tape, it cannot record the part of the body that only appears when a person uses it for the work they actually do. A tailor who has been fitting people for thirty years has learned to read the second kind of body, and that is the body the shirt actually has to fit.

What fading crafts like this preserve, I have come to believe, is not nostalgia but a slow kind of attention that scale cannot reproduce. A chain store can offer twenty sizes; it cannot offer one person looking at one customer for forty minutes. However, this kind of work is becoming harder to sustain. Bác Tâm's shop has two apprentices, but neither is sure they will inherit the business, because the rent rises every year and the foreigners who used to come for shirts increasingly buy online. If the workshop closes when he retires, what will be lost is not only a few shirts a month, but a very particular way of seeing a body — a kind of expertise that takes a lifetime to develop and a single decade of indifference to disappear.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect in passive form: 'had been measured'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I had expected to be measured the way I had been measured in every chain store I had visited before' kết hợp hai hình thức quá khứ hoàn thành (chủ động 'had expected', bị động 'had been measured') để diễn đạt cả kỳ vọng và trải nghiệm trước đó. Hình thức quá khứ hoàn thành bị động là 'had been + past participle'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I had expected to be measured the way I had been measured in every chain store I had visited before' combines active past perfect ('had expected') and passive past perfect ('had been measured') to describe both the expectation and the earlier experience. Past perfect passive = 'had been + past participle'.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal passive of past possibility: 'could have been captured'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'None of this could have been captured by a quick scan at a kiosk' dùng 'could have been + past participle' để diễn đạt khả năng quá khứ bị động (về mặt lý thuyết có thể, nhưng không xảy ra). Đây là dạng modal perfect bị động.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'None of this could have been captured by a quick scan at a kiosk' uses 'could have been + past participle' for past passive possibility (it was theoretically possible but did not happen). This is the modal perfect passive form.

sampleTranslationVi:
Lần đầu tiên tôi đến tiệm may của bác Tâm ở khu phố cổ Hà Nội, tôi đã mong được đo theo cách tôi đã được đo ở mọi cửa hàng chuỗi tôi từng đến trước đây — nhanh, ở ba chỗ, với hai cánh tay tôi giơ ra và một nhân viên cửa hàng lịch sự, hơi chán làm vài dấu nhỏ trên một tấm thẻ. Bác Tâm mất gần bốn mươi phút. Ông đo vai tôi ba lần, ở ba tư thế hơi khác nhau, bảo tôi đi qua phòng hai lần, và sau đó hỏi, bằng tiếng Anh chậm rãi, tôi thường làm loại công việc gì với cánh tay trong ngày. Khi tôi nói tôi dạy và viết, ông viết gì đó xuống một mảnh giấy nhỏ mà trông không giống một số đo chút nào. Hai tuần sau, khi tôi đến lấy áo, nó vừa theo cách tôi chưa bao giờ trải nghiệm trước đây. Vai phải cao hơn vai trái rất nhẹ, đúng theo cách vai tôi vốn vậy. Ông đã bù cho một sự khác biệt mà tôi thậm chí không biết về chính mình.

Điều tôi đã dần hiểu là các số đo của bác Tâm không thực sự là số đo theo nghĩa của cửa hàng chuỗi; chúng là một dạng quan sát chậm rãi về người tôi thực sự là. Ông đã đo vai tôi, nhưng ông cũng đã đo cách tôi đứng, cách tôi cử động cánh tay khi nói, và cách cổ áo sẽ nằm nếu tôi cúi đầu xuống để viết. Không điều nào trong số này có thể được ghi lại bởi một lần quét nhanh tại một quầy. Mặc dù một máy quét cơ thể hiện đại có thể ghi lại kích thước chính xác hơn nhiều so với thước dây, nó không thể ghi lại phần cơ thể chỉ xuất hiện khi một người dùng nó cho công việc họ thực sự làm. Một thợ may đã đo người trong ba mươi năm đã học cách đọc loại cơ thể thứ hai, và đó là cơ thể mà chiếc áo thực sự phải vừa.

Điều mà các nghề thủ công đang mai một như thế này bảo tồn, tôi đã dần tin, không phải là sự hoài niệm mà là một loại chú ý chậm rãi mà quy mô không thể tái tạo. Một cửa hàng chuỗi có thể cung cấp hai mươi cỡ; nó không thể cung cấp một người nhìn vào một khách hàng trong bốn mươi phút. Tuy nhiên, loại công việc này đang trở nên khó duy trì hơn. Tiệm của bác Tâm có hai người học việc, nhưng không ai chắc chắn sẽ kế thừa công việc, vì tiền thuê tăng mỗi năm và những người nước ngoài từng đến mua áo ngày càng mua online. Nếu xưởng đóng cửa khi ông về hưu, điều sẽ mất không chỉ là vài chiếc áo mỗi tháng, mà là một cách rất đặc biệt để nhìn cơ thể — một loại chuyên môn mất cả đời để phát triển và chỉ một thập kỷ thờ ơ là biến mất.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the past perfect passive correctly?
options: I had measured in every chain store I had visited before.
I have been measured in every chain store I had visited before.
I was been measured in every chain store I had visited before.
I had been measured in every chain store I had visited before.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect passive = 'had been + past participle': 'had been measured'.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect passive = 'had been + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'could have been + past participle' correctly?
options: None of this could have been captured by a quick scan at a kiosk.
None of this could have been capture by a quick scan at a kiosk.
None of this could be captured by a quick scan at a kiosk in the past.
None of this could had been captured by a quick scan at a kiosk.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past modal passive = 'could have been + past participle'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Could have been + past participle' for past passive possibility.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the present perfect correctly?
options: What I came to understand is that Bác Tâm's measurements were not really measurements in the chain-store sense.
What I have come to understand is that Bác Tâm's measurements were not really measurements in the chain-store sense.
What I have came to understand is that Bác Tâm's measurements were not really measurements in the chain-store sense.
What I have come understanding is that Bác Tâm's measurements were not really measurements in the chain-store sense.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have come' + 'to + base verb'; past participle of 'come' is 'come'.

options: Although a modern body-scanner can record dimensions far more precisely, but it cannot record the part of the body that only appears when a person uses it.
Although, a modern body-scanner can record dimensions far more precisely it cannot record the part of the body that only appears when a person uses it.
Although a modern body-scanner can record dimensions far more precisely, it cannot record the part of the body that only appears when a person uses it.
A modern body-scanner can record dimensions far more precisely, although however it cannot record the part of the body that only appears when a person uses it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses a relative clause?
options: The body what the shirt actually has to fit.
The body it the shirt actually has to fit.
The body that to the shirt actually has to fit.
The body the shirt actually has to fit.
mcQuestions.explanation: The relative pronoun ('that' / 'which') can be omitted when it is the object of the clause. 'What' is not a relative pronoun in this position.
mcQuestions.hint: Object relative pronouns can be omitted; 'what' is not used as a relative pronoun for things.

options: He had compensated for a difference I had not even known about myself.
He had compensated of a difference I had not even known about myself.
He had compensated to a difference I had not even known about myself.
He had been compensate for a difference I had not even known about myself.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Compensate for + noun' is the standard collocation; past perfect is 'had + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'become harder to + base verb' correctly?
options: This kind of work is becoming harder to sustaining.
This kind of work is becoming harder to sustain.
This kind of work is becoming harder for sustain.
This kind of work is becoming more hard to sustain.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Become harder to + base verb' is the standard structure. 'Harder' (comparative of 'hard') is correct; 'more hard' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: Comparative of 'hard' is 'harder'; 'to + base verb' after.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'neither is sure' correctly?
options: Bác Tâm's shop has two apprentices, but neither are sure they will inherit the business.
Bác Tâm's shop has two apprentices, but neither is sure they would inherit the business in the present.
Bác Tâm's shop has two apprentices, but neither is sure they will inherit the business.
Bác Tâm's shop has two apprentices, but neither is sure that they to inherit the business.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Neither' (alone) takes singular agreement in formal English. The future 'will inherit' is correct for a future possibility.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Neither' (alone) is grammatically singular in formal usage; use 'will + base' for future possibility.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'a kind of expertise that takes' correctly?
options: A kind of expertise what takes a lifetime to develop and a single decade of indifference to disappear.
A kind of expertise that take a lifetime to develop and a single decade of indifference to disappear.
A kind of expertise that takes a lifetime for develop and a single decade of indifference for disappear.
A kind of expertise that takes a lifetime to develop and a single decade of indifference to disappear.
mcQuestions.explanation: Relative 'that' with third-person singular verb ('takes'); 'take + period + to + base verb' is the correct collocation.
mcQuestions.hint: Relative 'that' (not 'what'); third-person agreement; 'take + period + to + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'have come to believe' correctly?
options: What fading crafts like this preserve, I have come to believe, is not nostalgia but a slow kind of attention.
What fading crafts like this preserve, I have came to believe, is not nostalgia but a slow kind of attention.
What fading crafts like this preserve, I came to believe, is not nostalgia but a slow kind of attention now.
What fading crafts like this preserve, I have come believing, is not nostalgia but a slow kind of attention.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have come' + 'to + base verb'. Past participle of 'come' is 'come', not 'came'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'not only … but …' correctly?
options: What will be lost is no only a few shirts a month, but a very particular way of seeing a body.
What will be lost is not only a few shirts a month, but a very particular way of seeing a body.
What will be lost is not only a few shirts a month, although a very particular way of seeing a body.
What will be lost is not only a few shirts a month, however a very particular way of seeing a body.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not only X, but Y' is the standard structure; 'no' instead of 'not' is wrong; 'although' and 'however' introduce different kinds of relationships.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Not only X, but Y' for cumulative emphasis.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If a craft can take a lifetime to develop but only a decade of indifference to disappear, what responsibility — if any — do customers, governments, and neighbours have to keep certain trades alive?
